data.title: CGDG: Dividend Growth ETF That Offers Global Protection
data.description:
Overview

With the continued pullback in the indices, it can be scary to add to positions right now. However, there are plenty of high-quality ETFs that offer better price resilience than the indices because of their core focus on dividend-paying companies. This is where the Capital Group Dividend Growers ETF (CGDG) comes in. CGDG not only provides exposure to some of the highest-quality dividend companies, but it also diversifies its focus to global companies with primary operations outside of the U.S. With rising global tensions, uncertainty around interest rates, and an uptick in unemployment, I wanted to provide some updated insights on CGDG and its overall value proposition.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that CGDG's share price has increased by nearly 15%, which is impressive considering the recent pullback in the market. When including all dividends paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 16.4% over the same time frame. CGDG now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 2%. While the starting yield isn't anything to get excited about, the magic is in the positive dividend growth potential over the next few years. I believe that patient investors will be able to see their annual dividend income effortlessly grow over time.

At the time of my last coverage, I issued a buy rating due to the global exposure away from the U.S. markets. Since then, the fund has outperformed the S&P 500 due to the recent pullback. At the time of my last coverage, I cited that the lack of exposure to specific growth companies could be a weakness. At the turn of the market cycle, it turns out that this weakness has actually become one of the reasons why CGDG can outperform over the next twelve months. If the start of 2026 is an indication of how the rest of the market will go, I believe that CGDG is an efficient holding for investors that may have a lower risk tolerance to uncertainty and volatility.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, CGDG has total assets under management of $4.46B that are spread across a diverse range of dividend-paying positions. The key differentiator in CGDG's strategy is the fact that it takes a multi-manager approach to building out its portfolio. This means there are different investment outlooks being compiled into the fund to prevent any sort of biases. So when one manager thinks to keep exposure to U.S. semiconductors, another manager may think to offset this with industrial exposure. Furthermore, the fund provides direct exposure to equities that have primary operations outside of the U.S. The portfolio's breakdown by region is as follows:

  • U.S. Equities: 50.8%
  • Non-U.S. Equities: 46.6%
  • Cash & Equivalents: 2.6%

When looking at the portfolio allocation by sector, we can see that CGDG is most heavily allocated to financials at 19.09% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to technology and industrials, which account for 12.58% and 11.91% of assets. The fund doesn't aim to mirror the S&P 500 and instead chooses to focus on high-quality dividend-paying companies. The selection process avoids companies that are considered yield traps. Therefore, management focuses on positions with attractive forecasted dividend growth potential, positive payout history, and an attractive current yield.

Since CGDG is actively managed, the fund has a higher-than-average expense ratio of 0.47%. However, this may be worth it if the fund is capable of outperforming during periods of uncertainty or declines. The actively managed approach means that there is no set rebalancing schedule. This means investors are relying on management being proactive enough to swap out underperformers and add defensive names when possible. I believe this approach is better than being passively locked into a fixed rebalancing schedule because this means that CGDG can rotate out of underperformers quickly.

When looking at the top holdings within CGDG, we can see that investors are getting exposure to some of the highest quality businesses in the world. For instance, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) is the largest individual position at 3.79% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to many other high-quality positions, including Broadcom (AVGO), Philip Morris International (PM), AstraZeneca (AZN), and Welltower (WELL), just to name a few. It's also notable that CGDG still provides an efficient allocation to technology companies that are capable of providing rapid growth and participating in the expansion of AI.

Defensive Outlook

When I last covered CGDG, I had an issue with the fact that it lacked exposure to meaningful growth positions, such as Alphabet (GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META). These are high-quality businesses that continue to rapidly grow earnings over time, and they pay out a small dividend. While these technology companies were taking off last year, the story has rapidly changed, and CGDG's lack of exposure has become one of its benefits. In fact, the selloff of these technology companies is one of the main drivers behind the pullback of the indices over the last few months.

Sentiment in the market changes rapidly and can be a powerful force. A year ago, any mention of Artificial Intelligence on an earnings call resulted in a stock trending higher. Today, the market doesn't have the same perspective on AI. Advances in the AI sector have grown so rapidly that investors are now concerned that the moat for many software companies is under the threat of being eliminated. When you add on the major capex spending levels, the market is now questioning whether or not these investments will actually lead to higher baseline growth in earnings.

To demonstrate the strength of CGDG's approach, we can measure its performance on a YTD basis. The State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) has declined by more than 7% on a YTD basis. However, CGDG has only declined by about 1% over the same time frame due to the lack of technology exposure. This proves that management's strategy for stock selection and rotating out of vulnerable positions can be effective. If the indices continue to experience headwinds, I anticipate that CGDG will continue to preserve capital for investors.

Furthermore, management was able to build a portfolio of positions that are undervalued from a price-to-earnings ratio. CGDG now trades with a price-to-earnings ratio of 17.4x, which undercuts the S&P 500's price-to-earnings ratio of ~27.16x. Furthermore, the portfolio has a sizeable allocation to defensive sectors like industrials, utilities, and consumer staples that tend to have easy-to-estimate cash flows through all market cycles. Investors may have been spoiled over the last decade; simply throw money at any of the Mag 7 companies and make a positive return. When the easy money is off the table, this is where CGDG has the potential to thrive.

Underperformance Risks

However, there's always the risk that the expansion of the AI market continues once these current macro challenges pass. Although the market is currently questioning the high capex spend, we already know that sentiment can change very quickly. If the large-cap technology leaders do in fact deliver substantial earnings growth, we can see the positive momentum continue.

For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can expand to a value of ~$3.5T by 2033. This represents an estimated CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% over the next few years. Even if this estimate is off by 50%, there's the chance that CGDG misses out on double-digit growth going forward. While CGDG does have some exposure through positions like TSM, it isn't structurally capable of fully participating in the upside growth.

Furthermore, the focus on sectors like financials and industrials means that the success of CGDG is sensitive to global economic growth. These are generally sectors that can be vulnerable to stagnation risks, such as lower consumer spending, infrastructure demand, and higher interest rates. The most notable barrier in the near term is the high-interest-rate environments that may lead to further deterioration within the debt markets. Despite the Fed initiating several cuts to interest rates throughout 2025, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range.

Even during the Fed's latest meeting, it was decided to leave interest rates unchanged. While higher rates may lead to higher net interest margins on loans, this also shrinks the spread between what it earns on loans and what banks pay on deposits. Furthermore, companies that are reliant on debt financing to fuel operations may be facing increased pressure as a result of the higher costs.

Dividend

CGDG now offers investors a starting dividend yield of roughly 2% while issuing those payouts on a quarterly basis. However, the actual payout amounts will vary every quarter. Historically, the largest payout is in the middle of the year. For instance, both of the fund's June distributions were at the highest end of its range. These varying payouts mean that CGDG may not be the best choice for retired investors seeking a consistent stream of income that can efficiently support lifestyle expenses. The smaller dividend yield and focus on dividend growers make CGDG a better position for investors that may be approaching retirement or have a lower risk tolerance. By allowing a longer holding period, this allows compound interest to work its magic.

The magic is in the ETF's ability to provide dividend growth over time. Although the fund's history is short, CGDG has managed to provide a trailing twelve-month dividend growth rate of 9.39%. So investors that have held CGDG since inception would now have a yield on cost around 2.8%. With a few more years of consecutive dividend growth, CGDG can easily allow long-term investors to obtain a yield on cost above 5%. When looking at the top holdings, it is clear that CGDG should be able to maintain an attractive rate of dividend growth going forward:

  • Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing: 5Y Dividend CAGR of 13.95%.
  • Broadcom: maintains a very healthy payout ratio of 34.11% with 15 consecutive years of growth without interruption.
  • Welltower: has a low payout ratio of 50.08%, which is extremely healthy for a REIT.

Lastly, CGDG provides the benefit of issuing tax-efficient dividends to investors. The ETF structure means that most of the distributions will be classified as qualified dividends, which are taxed at the most favorable rates for investors. However, the fund's inclusion of foreign investments means that investors may see those foreign taxes withheld from a portion of the distributions. This may enable the possibility to claim foreign tax credits if CGDG is held within a regular taxable account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on CGDG at this time. The fund's portfolio strategy has proven to be effective against the pullback of the indices. Although CGDG does have some technology exposure, management is very selective with what it holds, which has resulted in better capital preservation. Furthermore, the exclusion of high-growth, non-dividend-paying companies has worked out to its benefit. The ETF may be a great choice for investors that have a low-risk tolerance and want to offset uncertainty in the U.S. markets, higher interest rates, and a rotation out of technology. CGDG is also very likely to continue providing an attractive rate of dividend growth over the next few years.

data.title: The Market Is Early Calling The Peak In Micron
data.description:
I have been covering Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) since November last year. I remember concerns around high valuations and entering at highs at around $250, yet I felt a high conviction to Buy that was well supported. Similarly, at around $350, I felt the stock was becoming more volatile and the upside asymmetry was becoming riskier and hence recommended waiting for pullbacks for fresh positioning.

Micron, of course, belied expectations and rallied to over $470 before recent corrections brought it below prices where I had issued my downgrade. So, what does that mean for fresh entries at current levels, especially given my Hold rating was not too far off from levels we are seeing now? And more importantly, given the way Micron moves, $20-$30 moves are made within days, so the 5-10% net correction from the time of my last thesis may be stale even by the time this article gets published.

That means I have to answer the bigger question for the next big move - not a 5-10% target thesis that the gap between my last downgrade and current prices indicates. In this thesis, I try to answer whether Micron is likely to reach $400 earlier than $250 (enough room for the thesis to evolve beyond daily fluctuations). And this is what I find.

The demand story is a known support, and that continues to not be a factor in the near term, although long-term demand is a factor - it could actually prove that the cyclicality embedded in expectations today is more conservative and troughs in the AI era are higher than what has been seen earlier. A more important variable for Micron at the moment is the supply gap, and as of Q2 FY2026, even that remains supportive enough, as seen in pricing power and margin expansion. The only drag for Micron at the moment is a capex cyclicality risk and a guidance that conveys moderating incremental gains from here on (suggesting peak conditions are forming).

However, I feel that the timing for top formation and the amount of steam left in the cycle are being looked at conservatively. Operational constraints indicate supply cannot increase meaningfully in the near term, and there is enough time left for the supply to start responding in a way that starts to drag the pricing power. In other words, an opportunity seems to emerge from the way markets are correctly pricing in eventual normalization but potentially underestimating the trough depth and the duration over which elevated conditions can persist. That tells me we will hit $400 before we hit $250.

Further on in the thesis, I will discuss how the Buy trade is no longer as crowded as it was seemingly in January. Excess positioning has been removed, and volatility levels have compressed, which enables a fresh Buy not very far from the point of my last Hold. Interestingly, the thesis has evolved very quickly from Buy to a Hold and back to a Buy, all within a couple of months of each other - testimony to the magnitude of price moves Micron has been making of late.

A Recap Of Prior Calls

Not so long back in November last year, I felt markets were still mispricing the magnitude of demand and speed of earnings growth, alongside the supply constraints. I had modeled a DCF fair value of ~$380 but fully expected at least a few quarters to that target. What happened was growth was pulled forward, and margins also expanded beyond the near-term assumptions in my model. So, in January, I redrew the model with higher near-term growth assumptions and slower long-term growth numbers. Margins also had to be upgraded. The new fair value worked out to be around $420. I still called for a downgrade and advised waiting for fresh entries, although ~$70 of runway was implied as still left even as per my own model.

My downgrade to a Hold is therefore a reflection of how the thesis has moved from a high-conviction entry thesis to a mature and fulfilled thesis. Returns will come potentially with time and cash generation rather than rerating. The business may continue to exceed expectations, and momentum may still push Micron further up, but the price-value framework (relatively saturated now—within two months of the Buy) indicates the stock may not fundamentally price in any further execution surprises.

The Crowded Trade And Top Formation

One of the core reasons for my discomfort around the ~$350 levels was that the Buy trade was becoming more crowded, and Micron had started to make volatile and sharp big moves almost on a daily level, and on both sides. The 30-day rolling volatility chart below shows how, barring the close to 100% volatility levels seen around April 2025 (when Micron was less crowded below $100 and also going through a wider market volatility due to tariff uncertainties), the overall volatility trends were already in an uptrend by November last year (when I still found enough upside asymmetry to still issue a Buy). By December-January, not only had the asymmetry shrunk (a fair value of ~$380 as per my older model and ~$420 as per an upgraded model), but volatility levels had also shot up meaningfully from ~60% to ~75%. The combined effect of shrinking upside asymmetry and rising volatility was a no-go.

Another chart that highlights how the volatility and the magnitude of intraday moves have sharply increased over the past year is shown below. This one plots the absolute close-to-close % change in daily prices. This shows how the daily changes had moved from just over 2% a year ago to just under 4% by January. That amount of intraday moves and a rising 30-day volatility trend is often an indicator of a top forming in share prices and indicates crowded trades rather than fundamentals and long-term trends forming, in my view. In the recent sell-off, the volatility levels are back to ~60%, and on the way down, this is a lagging indicator - i.e. actual volatility should be lower than what we were seeing in November last year. This development removes one of the hurdles I had for fresh Buying at $350 in January this year. The rest of the asymmetry thesis is still pending, though.

Ditching The DCF Framework

The DCF modeling approach was still a reasonable approach in November because the forward distribution of outcome seemed linear and multiple quarters out. What has changed since is that both growth and margins have been pulled forward sharply, compressing the timing from a multi-quarter and multi-year cycle into a few concentrated quarters. As peak conditions start forming, cash flows are no longer smooth in the near term. Timing dependencies and terminal values are sensitive assumptions that affect the near-term share move versus a fair value estimate. Therefore, I move to a more qualitative thesis now with emphasis on the cycle duration outlook and supply-demand imbalance to see whether a Buy thesis now carries an edge over what the market is pricing in.

Where The Markets Are Potentially Wrong

The current consensus shows expectations of earnings peaking only in 2027. The quarterly rundown of that trajectory shows real slowdown starting to show up only by Q2 FY2027 (4 quarters away) and negative EPS growth from a year after. This assumes both a sharp demand trough (going as deep as ~$30 in the long term, by FY2029) as the AI capex cycle normalizes within those timeframes and a supply catching up with demand scenario.

My take is that the markets are correct on the direction but potentially wrong on the magnitude of normalization and the timing. Looking at the operational data and other indicators and how they have moved between Q1 and Q2 FY2026 helps understand why. Starting with pricing, because this is where the signal is cleanest in my view. DRAM prices were already reported to be strongly up by ~20% in Q1, but they have gone up much more sharply in Q2. DRAM prices were up by well over 60%, and NAND prices were reported to be up by a high-70% range. While a backward-looking indicator, it still shows that the supply imbalance is still extremely strong. Stronger pricing power has also meant gross margins have expanded significantly from ~57% in Q1 to ~75% now.

The forward outlook on pricing power and what is required for Micron to surprise the markets depends on the two important age-old variables - demand and supply. The demand thesis does not need an introduction for Micron. Management commentary in Q2 indicated they were able to meet 50% to two-thirds of the demand from key customers. Existing unmet demand is sufficient to keep the demand tight enough for pricing power. The supply side is what needs monitoring because that becomes the binding constraint.

On the supply side, Q2 commentary indicates that cleanroom limitations and node transitions continue to drag supply expansion bids. Management also said that industry supply could remain short of demand well beyond calendar year 2026. This in itself could mean the current trajectory assumes a sharp compression in EPS growth levels by Q2 FY2027. Capex trends show continued investments in FY2026 with expectations of above $25b for the year. However, with the current run rate, when extrapolated, it implies those levels roughly, so it is not a material ramp. FY2027 is expected to be a heavier ramp with a ramp of ~$10b in construction capex. The Idaho fab is expected to start initial wafer output only by mid-2027, and I believe other additions are even further out, supporting my immediate tight supply thesis - tight enough for longer to defy market expectations of a deceleration in EPS growth in 4 quarters from now.

The Q3 margin guidance of ~81% is still a further expansion, pushing Micron toward peak margin territory. And management has indicated moderating gains from there on. However, it does not affect the EPS trajectory assumed in the next few quarters. Capacity additions in the memory industry are a multi-year lead-time project, and immediate efficiency gains and higher production based on technology transitions have limited capacity to scale and address the magnitude of supply shortage at play. As the supply tightness looks worse than assumed, the EPS trajectory ahead will be able to remain strong even if pricing power and further margin gains remain limited. I expect volume growth (bit shipments still growing), mix shift (HBM and high-value products), and operating leverage to remain supportive.

Another potential mismatch in the market's long-term trough and terminal value assumptions could be the expected trough of ~$30 by FY2029 (from peak EPS of ~$100). This sharp drop potentially looks at an end date for the current capex cycle for training clusters. However, the extent of demand for inference loads is still speculative (and conservative looking at the sharp drop in EPS expectations). Depending on the number of users and queries, memory intensity is likely to rise per workload over time. Demand could also spill over from a handful of large hyperscalers to a broader base of users, devices, and enterprises. That upside of a consistent post-build phase is potentially not being factored into the cyclical EPS expectation model we have today.

Why $400 Before $250

Overall, for prices to go down further, we will need a multiple compression trajectory as earnings grow into valuations - going as sharply down as ~3x in forward earnings terms by FY2029. If we assume even a reasonable ~25% multiple expansion (~3.66x earnings), we get to $400 on multiple rerating alone. What actually supports an even higher target is an earnings surprise trajectory that extends beyond the next 4 quarters (as modeled today). If that happens, the upside opens up meaningfully well beyond $400. That path is highly possible looking at how the supply remains constrained longer than expected, a strong demand environment (strong enough scenarios also exist that see inference loads turning out stronger than the current cyclicality embedded in expectations). This path does not need much margin expansion or further gains in terms of pricing power. That path also enables some multiple expansion too, doubling the impact on returns.

The path to $200 needs a valuation sentiment drop due to a risk-off macro environment - that could probably still happen, but current forward valuations appear too low and require a lot of intrinsic factors to also play out. Supply should increase meaningfully, demand should not persist the way markets are factoring in, and margins should fall meaningfully too - something not indicated by the state we are in at the moment.
